article_title: I replaced my entire workflow with Claude Code, and I'm not even a developer
core_thesis: Claude Code, an AI coding tool from Anthropic, can replace an entire creative and research workflow even for non-developers by using natural language prompts, MCP servers, and markdown files to automate ideation, research, and content structuring, thereby reducing friction in the creative process.
detailed_summary: The author, a tech journalist, initially dismissed Claude Code because they are not a developer and already used other AI tools (Perplexity, local LLMs). However, after experimenting, they found Claude Code far more versatile for their workflow. By attaching Claude Code to MCP servers for real-time web and social media search, and using a CLAUDE.md file to define user archetypes and technical sources, they can generate article ideas, research topics, and outline sections without writing any code. The tool's ability to create files and folders automatically keeps the author organized. They describe a typical workflow: role-playing a prompt to get five article ideas, then expanding on selected ideas using context from documentation (e.g., via Context7). The author is on the Max plan but notes their usage is minimal compared to a developer's. They plan to create subagents to further automate the process, emphasizing that agents are defined in plain language, not code. The core insight is that Claude Code's power lies in its natural language interface, making it accessible to non-developers for creative and research tasks.

entities.name: Joe Rice-Jones
entities.role: Author and tech journalist who replaced his workflow with Claude Code

entities.name: Claude Code
entities.role: AI coding tool from Anthropic used for ideation, research, and content creation

entities.name: MCP servers
entities.role: Model Context Protocol servers that provide Claude Code with up-to-date information and social media data

entities.name: CLAUDE.md
entities.role: Configuration file that defines user archetypes, technical sources, and preferences for Claude Code

entities.name: Context7
entities.role: Tool used to read code documentation and provide context to Claude Code

key_data_points: The author replaced a stack of Perplexity for research and a local LLM for summarization with a single tool: Claude Code (🟢 relevant to your productivity: consolidating tools can streamline workflows).

Claude Code can be attached to MCP servers to search for real-time information and social media posts, enabling rapid ideation without manual browsing (🟢 relevant to your research: automates trend discovery).
A CLAUDE.md file allows the user to define technical sources and user archetypes, tailoring ideation to specific audiences (🟢 relevant to your content creation: personalizes output).

The author uses role-play prompts to generate article ideas, e.g., 'You are my technical writing partner... propose 5 specific article ideas' (🟢 relevant to your prompting: structured prompts yield better results).
Claude Code automatically creates folders and markdown files for each project, keeping the workflow organized (🟢 relevant to your homelab/devops: file management automation).
The author plans to create subagents for search, summarization, and orchestration, all defined in plain language without code (🟢 relevant to your ambition: agentic workflows are accessible).
The author is on the Max plan but uses few tokens compared to a developer, suggesting the tool is cost-effective for non-coding tasks (🟢 relevant to your student budget: efficient use of resources).
methodology_steps: 1. Set up Claude Code with MCP servers for web search and social media access to get real-time data.
2. Create a CLAUDE.md file that defines user archetypes (e.g., average user, power user) and preferred technical sources (e.g., Proxmox documentation).

3. Use a role-play prompt to generate article ideas: 'You are my technical writing partner. Using CLAUDE.md, propose 5 specific article ideas that would be valuable for [userType] using [tool1] and [tool2] at home. For each idea, include: Working title, One-sentence “why this matters”, 3–5 bullet points of key sections.'
4. Select promising ideas and ask Claude Code to expand them using context from relevant documentation (e.g., via Context7).
5. Let Claude Code create a new folder and markdown file for each expanded idea to keep work organized.
6. (Optional) Split the workflow into subagents: one for search/download, one for summarization, and an orchestrator agent to automate daily ideation.
conclusions: Claude Code can replace multiple AI tools for non-developers by handling ideation, research, and content structuring through natural language prompts and markdown files. The author's experience shows that coding knowledge is not required to leverage the tool's full potential.

The use of MCP servers and CLAUDE.md files enables personalized, real-time research that reduces the time spent on manual browsing. This approach can be applied to any field requiring trend analysis or content planning.

The planned agentic workflow (subagents for search, summarization, orchestration) demonstrates a scalable, automated system for daily content generation. Since agents are defined in plain language, this is accessible to non-programmers.
personal_relevance_summary: This article is highly relevant to you as a programmer and cybersecurity enthusiast because it demonstrates how to use Claude Code for non-coding tasks like research and ideation, which can free up time for coding and red teaming. The described workflow—using MCP servers for real-time data and CLAUDE.md for personalization—can be adapted to monitor security vulnerabilities, GitHub repos, or threat intelligence feeds. As a student, the productivity gains from automating research and content structuring can help you manage coursework and side projects. The article also provides a concrete example of building agentic workflows without code, which aligns with your ambition to automate and optimize your processes.
extra_advice: Consider setting up a similar Claude Code workflow for red teaming: create a CLAUDE.md with your preferred CVE databases, exploit repositories, and threat actor profiles. Use MCP servers to pull the latest vulnerabilities and social media discussions. Then, prompt Claude Code to generate attack scenarios or research questions for your next engagement. This can automate the reconnaissance phase and keep you ahead of emerging threats.
